title: Quick check: Phrase and exact match

questions.prompt: When does exact match still beat broad match?

choices.text: Brand defense (exact match on your brand name in a separate brand campaign), specific durable high-converting concepts you've validated, compliance-restricted verticals (crypto, supplements, financial), localized small-volume queries, and specific competitor brand bidding where legally OK. Even then 'exact' isn't literal — Google applies semantic variation to exact match too. The 80/20 hybrid pattern in mature accounts: ~80% broad, ~15% phrase, ~5% exact.

questions.explanation: Brand defense, durable specific concepts, compliance, narrow local.

questions.prompt: Account has brand keywords mixed in with general product keywords in one campaign. Why split?

choices.text: Brand keyword performance is fundamentally different (very high CTR, very high conversion rate, often non-incremental). Mixing brand with non-brand distorts campaign-level metrics and prevents you from running incrementality tests on brand spend. Standard pattern: separate 'Brand - Exact' campaign with exact-match brand keywords, aggressive bid willingness, conservative tROAS or tCPA, and negatives for things like '[brand] login' if you don't want to bid on existing-customer queries. Lets you treat brand budget as a strategic decision separate from acquisition.

questions.explanation: Brand and non-brand are different jobs; separate campaigns isolate them.
